coredata.dc:title: Highly porous defective carbons derived from seaweed biomass as efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction in both alkaline and acidic media
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yajuan
Zhang, Xu
Yang, Qifeng
Chen, Kai
Guo, Jun
Zhou, Dongying
Feng, Lai
Slanina, Zdeněk
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report a series of defective carbon catalysts prepared through a facile and scalable “N-doping-removal” process using seaweed biomass sodium alginate (SA) as precursor. Our systematic studies reveal that the defect content, porosity characteristic and conductivity of defective carbons can be finely tuned by manipulating the pyrolysis temperature and viscosity of precursor polymer SA, which significantly affect the ORR performance. The optimized defective porous carbon catalyst (i.e., D-PC-1(900)) that was revealed to possess abundant ORR-active defects, large surface area of 1377 m2g-1, abundant hierarchical porosity and good conductivity, exhibited very nice ORR activity and selectivity in 0.1 M KOH, comparable to the commercial Pt/C catalyst. In 0.5 M H2SO4, considerable ORR activity was also observed for D-PC-1(900), which is among the highest reported for defective carbons and comparable to many of N-doped carbons. Density functional theory calculations indicate that the carbon defect can create the active sites for ORR in acidic media. More importantly, in both alkaline and acidic media, D-PC-1(900) shows much better stability and methanol tolerance than those of the Pt/C catalyst. All these results demonstrate that the seaweed biomass derived defective carbon is an excellent candidate for non-precious-metal ORR catalyst in various fuel cells.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Long-term stability of defective carbon catalysts

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Suzhou Key Laboratory for Advanced Carbon Materials and Wearable Energy Technologies

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported in part by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51372158 , 51772195 ) and Suzhou Key Laboratory for Advanced Carbon Materials and Wearable Energy Technologies .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of a typical “N-doping-removal” process.

(a) CV curves of D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900) compared with Pt/C (40 μgPt cm−2) in O2 (red) or N2-saturated (black) 0.1 M KOH. (b) LSV curves of D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900) compared with Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at 1600 rpm. (c) LSV curves of D-PC-1(900) at different rotation speeds (rpm) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. (d) Koutecky−Levich (K–L) plots of D-PC-1(900) with calculated electron transfer number (n) given after @. (e) Plots of HO
2
− yield (up) and n value (down) against electrode potential for D-PC-1(900) and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. (f) Tafel plot of D-PC-1(900) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH compared with that of Pt/C. (Catalyst loading is 400 μg cm-2, while Pt loading is 40 μgPt cm−2.) (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) LSV curves of D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900) compared with Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4 at 1600 rpm. (b) LSV curves of D-PC-1(900) at different rotation speeds (rpm) in 0.5 M H2SO4. (c) Koutecky−Levich (K–L) plots of D-PC-1(900) with calculated electron transfer number (n) given after @. (d) Plots of HO
2
− yield (up) and n value (down) against electrode potential for D-PC-1(900) compared with those of Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4. (d) Tafel plot of D-PC-1(900) compared with that of Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4. (Catalyst loading is 400 μg cm-2, while Pt loading is 40 μgPt cm−2.) (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a,b) XRD and Raman spectra of D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900). (c,d) TEM and HR-TEM images of D-PC-1(900). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of D-PC-1(900), D-PC-1(800), D-PC-1(700), D-PC-2(900) and D-PC-3(900). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS survey spectra of N-C-1, D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900). The highlighted area was enlarged in the right panel. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-3(900) measured at their onset potential in (a) O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution and (b) 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, respectively. Inset shows equivalent circuit used to interpret the data. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic pathways toward the formation of A-O-OH+. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a,b) LSV curves of D-PC-1(900) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions, respectively, before and after 5000 or 2000 CV cycles between 0.6-1.0 V and 0.4–0.8 V vs RHE. (c,d) i–t chronoamperometric responses of the catalyst-modified electrodes upon the addition of methanol at 0.6 V (vs RHE) with a rotating speed of 1600 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. The arrow indicates the addition of methanol. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Surface areas (SBET) were calculated by the BET method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Micropore areas (Smicro) were calculated using the t-plot method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mesopore areas (Smeso) were calculated from the difference of the BET surface area and micropore area (Smeso = SBET-Smicro).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impedance spectra fitting results for the ORR of D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-3(900) in alkaline and acidic media, respectively.

head.ce:title: Highly porous defective carbons derived from seaweed biomass as efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction in both alkaline and acidic media

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Soochow Institute for Energy and Materials InnovationS (SIEMIS), College of Physics, Optoelectronics and Energy & Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Soochow Institute for Energy and Materials InnovationS (SIEMIS)
Optoelectronics and Energy & Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of Arizona, Tucson, AZ, 85721-0041, United States.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report a series of defective carbon catalysts prepared through a facile and scalable “N-doping-removal” process using seaweed biomass sodium alginate (SA) as precursor. Our systematic studies reveal that the defect content, porosity characteristic and conductivity of defective carbons can be finely tuned by manipulating the pyrolysis temperature and viscosity of precursor polymer SA, which significantly affect the ORR performance. The optimized defective porous carbon catalyst (i.e., D-PC-1(900)) that was revealed to possess abundant ORR-active defects, large surface area of 1377 m2g-1, abundant hierarchical porosity and good conductivity, exhibited very nice ORR activity and selectivity in 0.1 M KOH, comparable to the commercial Pt/C catalyst. In 0.5 M H2SO4, considerable ORR activity was also observed for D-PC-1(900), which is among the highest reported for defective carbons and comparable to many of N-doped carbons. Density functional theory calculations indicate that the carbon defect can create the active sites for ORR in acidic media. More importantly, in both alkaline and acidic media, D-PC-1(900) shows much better stability and methanol tolerance than those of the Pt/C catalyst. All these results demonstrate that the seaweed biomass derived defective carbon is an excellent candidate for non-precious-metal ORR catalyst in various fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, platinum (Pt)-based catalysts have been widely used as very efficient electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reactions (ORR) in both alkaline and acidic media [1,2], which are key reactions in various fuel cells [3,4]. However, Pt-based catalysts usually suffer from several unfavorable problems [5,6], such as high-cost, poor durability, low tolerance to methanol (MeOH) crossover and CO poisoning effects, which impede their practical applications in fuel cells. To solve these bottleneck problems, low-cost and Pt-free catalysts with high catalytic activity and superior stability are highly desired. Thus, in recent years, a variety of carbon-based catalysts have been developed as inexpensive alternatives to Pt-based catalysts for ORR [7].

It has been well established that the catalytic performances of carbon catalysts can be significantly improved by using the heteroatom-doping strategy. It has been reported that a variety of heteroatoms such as N [8,9], P [10,11], S [12,13], B [14,15] can be homogeneously doped into porous carbons, leading to the introduction of new catalytic centers. In recent years, there have been numerous reports of the heteroatom-doped porous carbons with high catalytic activities, which are even comparable or superior to those of Pt/C catalyst [16–19]. On the other hand, it has been long believed that pristine or undoped carbons display very poor ORR catalytic activities.

However, a series of studies emerging very recently suggested a new mechanism, in which ORR is related to the specific carbon defects, such as edge pentagons, 585 and 7557 motifs [20–27]. Nevertheless, there are only a limited number of cases reporting promising results, including undoped graphene quantum dots/graphene nanoribbons (GQDs/GNRs) hybrid [24], defective graphene (D-G) [27] and defective activated carbons (D-AC) [26]. These carbon catalysts demonstrated excellent or considerable ORR performance under alkaline conditions, which is more or less comparable to that of commercial Pt/C catalyst, indicating their promising applications in the field of fuel cells.

Continuing with the exploitation of the potential of undoped carbons as ORR catalysts, we herein report a new class of defective carbon catalysts, which is prepared using seaweed biomass derived sodium alginate (SA) as precursor through a facile and scalable “N-doping-removal” process (see experimental section). The optimized defective carbon exhibits good ORR catalytic activities as well as high selectivity and stability in both alkaline and acidic media, which are even comparable or superior to those of commercial Pt/C as well as most heteroatom-doped porous carbons. The excellent catalytic performance has been clearly attributed to the defects generated through the N-doping and removal process. This work hopefully provides a promising strategy to develop efficient and low-cost defective carbon electrocatalysts in both alkaline and acidic media.

Sodium alginate is chosen as precursor for the synthesis of defective carbon ORR catalysts based on the following considerations: (i) SA derived from abundant seaweed biomass is cheap, green and readily available; (ii) SA bears abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which can be removed from the polymeric matrix through pyrolysis, resulting in 3D carbon materials with high porosity and good electrical conductivity [28–31]; (iii) SA can be chemically modified under mild conditions, which allows N-doping at low temperature (<200 °C).

Fig. 1 depicts the typical “N-doping-removal” route employed for the preparation of defective carbons. As illustrated, N atoms were doped in carbon matrix and then removed to create ORR active defects. To prepare N-doped carbon, precursor polymer SA was hydrothermally carbonized in the presence of NH3 .H2O (28%). In a typical preparation, 2.0 g of SA was dissolved in a mixed solution containing 120 mL deionized H2O and 40 mL NH3 .H2O (28%). Then, the mixture underwent a hydrothermal carbonization at 160 °C for 4 h, yielding a dark brown solution containing N-doped carbon quantum dots (see TEM image in Fig. S1). The resulting solution was neutralized with HCl followed by a dialysis (1000KD) for 2 days to completely remove the salts (i.e., NH4Cl and NaCl). This solution was freeze-dried for at least 24 h to produce N-doped carbon powder. When SA with medium viscosity (i.e., 200–500 mPa s) was used as precursor, the hydrothermal product is denoted as N-C-1. Alternatively, when SA with low viscosity (i.e., 100–200 mPa s) was used, the product is denoted as N-C-3.

Finally, defective carbons (i.e., D-PC-1(x) and D-PC-3(900), x = 700, 800 and 900) were obtained by pyrolyzing N-C-1 or N-C-3 at high temperature (i.e., 700–900 °C) under Ar/H2(5%) atmosphere, respectively. For a typical pyrolysis, the dried N-C-1 or N-C-3 was placed in the center of a quartz tube furnace. After pumping and purging the system with Ar/H2(5%) 3 × , the temperature was raised to 200 °C with a heating rate of 3 °C min−1 and maintained for 2 h. Then, the temperature was further raised to 900 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 and held for 30 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the product was washed with water and dried for further studies.

For comparison, a control catalyst was prepared via a one-step pyrolysis of SA with medium viscosity using SiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) with an average size of 15 nm as hard template and denoted as D-PC-2(900). In a typical preparation, SA and SiO2 NPs were mixed in a water solution with a mass ratio of 80:1 and subjected to freeze-drying. The dry mixture was pyrolyzed at 900 °C for 30 min under Ar/H2 (5%) atmosphere. Finally, the SiO2 NPs were removed by washing with 0.1 M HF, yielding the product of D-PC-2(900).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku D/Max 2000 powder diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 20 mA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were carried out with an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer using a monochromated Al Kα excitation source. Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured on a Varian CP-3800 spectrometer in the wavenumber range of 400–4000 nm−1. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) observations were carried out with a Hitachi HT7700 and a FEI Tecnai F20, respectively. Raman spectra were recorded on a Micro-Raman spectroscopy system (Renishaw in Via-reflex, 633 nm excitation laser). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77 K were measured on an ASAP 2020 volumetric adsorption analyzer (Micromeritics instrument Co.). All samples were degassed at 473 K overnight prior to adsorption measurements. A part of the N2 sorption isotherm in the P/P
0 range of 0.005–0.05 was fitted to the BET equation to estimate the BET surface area. The pore size distribution was obtained using nonlocal density functional theory (NL-DFT) model and assuming slit pore geometry. Micropore area (Smicro) was calculated using t-plot method.

All electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature on a potentiostat (CHI 760E, CH Instrument, Shanghai, China) and a rotating ring disk electrode system (RRDE-3A, ALS Co., Ltd, Japan) with a standard three electrode cell. A Pt wire and an Hg/HgO/1 M OH− electrode (0.14 V vs NHE) or an Ag/AgCl/saturated KCl solution electrode (0.1988 V vs NHE) were used as the counter and reference electrode, respectively. A RDE with glassy carbon (GC) disk electrode (4 mm in diameter) and a rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE, ALS Co., Ltd, Japan) with a Pt ring (5 mm inner diameter and 7 mm outer diameter) and a GC disk (4 mm diameter) are used as the substrate for the working electrodes. Before use, the GC electrodes in RDE/RRDE are polished using aqueous alumina suspension on polishing pad. In alkaline medium (0.1 M KOH), the potentials measured against Hg/HgO/1 M OH− electrode, was converted to the potential versus the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to E RHE = E (Hg/HgO/1M OH-)+0.0591 pH + E
0
(Hg/HgO/1M OH-) [32]. In acidic medium (0.5 M H2SO4), the potentials measured against Ag/AgCl/saturated KCl solution electrode, was converted to the potential versus RHE according to E RHE = E (Ag/AgCl/saturated KCl solution)+0.0591 pH + E
0
(Ag/AgCl/saturated KCl solution) [33].

To fabricate catalyst-modified working electrode, the catalyst ink was prepared by ultrasonically mixing 5 mg carbon catalyst with 95 μL Nafion (5 wt%) solution and 350 μL ethanol. As-prepared catalyst ink was casted onto the fresh GC surface of RDE or RRDE. Pt/C catalyst (10 wt% Pt on graphitized carbon, Sigma-Aldrich) ink was similarly prepared. Carbon-catalyst-loading is 400 μμμg cm−2, while Pt/C-catalyst-loading is 40 μgPt cm−2.

ORR performance of the catalysts was investigated via cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions, respectively. The scan rate was 25 mV s-1 for CV and 5 mV s-1 for LSV tests. Oxygen reduction current was evaluated by subtracting the background capacitive current, which was measured by scanning the electrode in a N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH or 0.5 M H2SO4 solution under the same conditions. The electron transfer number (n) was calculated using Koutecky−Levich (K-L) equations [34]:
(1)
1 J
=
1
J K
+
1
J L
=
1
J K
+
1
B
ω
1 / 2
(2)
B = 0.20 n F C o D
o
2 / 3
ν
− 1 / 6
where J is the measured current density, J
k is the kinetic current density, B is the Levich constant, ω is the angular velocity of the rotating electrode, n is the overall number of electrons transferred in the ORR process, F is the Faraday constant (96485 C mol-1), and ν is the kinetic viscosity (0.01 cm2 s-1) of the electrolyte. C
0 is the bulk concentration (1.26 × 10−6 mol cm-3 in 0.1 M KOH and 1.13 × 10−6 mol cm-3 in 0.5 M H2SO4) of O2, D
0 is the diffusion coefficient (1.93 × 10−5 cm2 s-1 in 0.1 M KOH and 1.8 × 10−5 cm2 s-1 in 0.5 M H2SO4) of O2 [35,36].

Rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) tests for ORR were measured with a scan rate of 5 mV s-1, and the ring potential was set at 1.2–1.4 V (vs. RHE) for H2O2 production in the O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions, respectively. The electron transfer number n and the production yield of HO2 − in 0.1 M KOH or H2O2 in 0.5 M H2SO4 (HO2 −% or H2O2%) were determined using the following equations:
(3)
H
O 2 −
% or
H 2
O 2
% = 200 ×
I r
/ N
I d +
I r
/ N
(4)
n = 4 ×
I d
I d +
I r
/ N
where I
d is the disk current, I
r is the ring current, and N is the current collection efficiency of the Pt ring, which is 0.424 provided by the manufacturer.

For stability tests, the i−t chronoamperometric measurements were performed in either O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH or 0.5 M H2SO4 at 0.6 V (vs RHE) for 2.5–5.5 h with a rotation rate of 400 rpm. For methanol crossover effect tests, the i−t chronoamperometric response at 0.6 V (vs. RHE) was recorded by RDE tests with a rotation rate of 1600 rpm at the same potentials, and followed by the introduction of methanol (3 M).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out in 0.1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively, on an IM6e Electrochemical Workstation (ZAHNER, Germany) with the frequency sweeping from 50 000–0.01 Hz, where the AC voltage amplitude was set at 5 mV. Before EIS scanning, the electrode was equilibrated at the corresponding bias potential for 120 s.

To estimate the electron conductivities of carbon catalysts, four-probe measurements were performed with a ST2263 Double Testing Digital Four-probe Tester (Suzhou Jingge Electronic Co. Ltd.) using Au-coated copper electrodes at room temperature. Before measurement, 5 mg carbon catalyst and 5 mg acetylene black were mixed with 190 μL Nafion (5 wt%) solution and 700 μL ethanol. Then, the mixture was deposited on a glass substrate by spin-coating to form a 450-nm-thick film. The configuration of four-point probes in-line was performed with probe spacing of 2 mm, and a constant force was applied to the probes to make sure of good contact between probes and sample.

ORR catalytic activities of these as-prepared carbon catalysts were evaluated by means of cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in N2 and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CV of carbon catalysts measured in N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH show no reduction peak, while those measured in O2-saturated solvent show evident ORR peaks, indicating that all these carbon catalysts are ORR active in alkaline medium. Furthermore, as suggested by LSV measurements (Fig. S2 and Fig. 2b), the precursor N-C-1 shows very poor ORR activity, whereas D-PC-1(900) exhibits the highest ORR activity in terms of an onset potential (E
onset ) of 1.01 V, a half-wave potential (E
1/2 ) of 0.83 V and a limiting current density (J
L ) of −5.43 mA cm−2. All these values are very comparable to those of commercial Pt/C catalyst (E
onset : 1.01 V, E
1/2 : 0.84 V and J
L : −5.35 mA cm−2, all close to the reference data with similar Pt loading) [17,37,38]. It is also noteworthy that the ORR activity of D-PC-1(900) is even comparable to previously reported typical carbon catalysts with N-dopants (i.e., CNC700 [22], SNC [39] and 1DPC-L3 [40]), though it is still inferior to the state-of-the-art N-doped carbon catalysts (see Table S1). In contrast, the ORR activity of D-PC-2(900) is among the poorest, though it was similarly prepared but without the “N-doping-removal” process. As for other carbon catalysts, though D-PC-1(800) and D-PC-3(900) prepared with N-doping-removal process are slightly inferior to D-PC-1(900), they are much better than D-PC-2(900). These results indicate that the “N-doping-removal” process is essential in achieving the defective carbons with high ORR activity.

To determine whether the ORR on D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900) occur through a four-electron pathway and compare with Pt/C, LSV curves at different rotation rates were measured (Fig. 2c, FigsS3a, S4a). Based on the K-L plots (J −1 vs. ω
−1/2 in Fig. 2d, Figs. S3b, S4b), the electron-transfer number (n) was calculated to be 3.87–3.98, 2.64–3.12, 3.96–4.0 in a potential range of 0.50–0.70 V (vs. RHE) for D-PC-1(900), D-PC-2(900) and Pt/C, respectively. These results were further validated by rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) measurements (see Fig. S5). Based on the measured ring current and disk current, the electron-transfer number was calculated to be 3.86–4.0 for D-PC-1(900) in a potential range of 0.40–0.75 V (vs. RHE, see Fig. 2e and Table S2). These values are in good agreement with the K-L results, suggesting a four-electron pathway dominant in the ORR on D-PC-1(900) in alkaline medium. In addition, the RRDE measurements reveal that the production of HO
2
− is highly suppressed on D-PC-1(900) with a yield below ∼6.9% over the scanned potential range (0.40–0.75 V), almost comparable to that obtained on the Pt/C (a value of ∼3.8%, close to the reference data) [41]. Thus, these results suggest that D-PC-1(900) shows a high ORR catalytic efficiency in alkaline medium. Moreover, D-PC-1(900) demonstrates a Tafel slope of 90.0 mV dec−1 (see Fig. 2f), very close to that of Pt/C (88.7 mV dec−1), indicating that the ORR kinetics of D-PC-1(900) is similar to that of Pt/C.

The ORR catalytic activities of these carbon catalysts were also tested in acidic medium (0.5 M H2SO4), and similar results were obtained. As shown in Fig. 3 a, D-PC-1(900) exhibits a considerable ORR activity in terms of an E
onset of 0.82 V, an E
1/2 of 0.60 V and a high J
L of −4.60 mA cm−2, followed by D-PC-1(800) with an E
1/2 of 0.63 V and an E
onset of 0.80 V. As compared to Pt/C catalyst, the E
onset of D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-1(800) are almost comparable, whereas their E
1/2 are negatively shifted by ca. 100–130 mV. The E
onset and E
1/2 of D-PC-1(900) are also comparable to or even better than those reported for the typical carbon catalysts with N-dopants (see Table S3). These results demonstrate that D-PC-1(900) is among the best of reported carbon catalysts in acidic medium. Additionally, Fig. 3b gives the LSV curves of D-PC-1(900) measured at various rotating rates, showing rapidly increased current densities with the increase in rotation speed. The electron transfer number (n) was calculated to be 3.72–3.81 in a potential range of 0.3–0.5 V (vs. RHE) using K-L plots (Fig. 3c), very close to those (3.9–4.0) calculated for Pt/C (Fig. S6). This result was confirmed by RRDE measurements (Fig. S7 and Table S4), which suggest an electron transfer number (n) of 3.86–3.94 and a H2O2 yield of 2.9–7.2% in the similar potential range (i.e., 0.20–0.55 V, see Fig. 3d). Thus, the combined study of RDE and RRDE suggests a four-electron pathway dominated in the ORR on D-PC-1(900) in acidic medium. As shown in Fig. 3e, the Tafel slope of D-PC-1(900) is calculated to be 56.8 mV dec−1 at low potentials, close to that of Pt/C (53.8 mV dec−1), indicating their similar ORR kinetics in acidic medium. Noteworthy is that D-PC-2(900) shows almost negligible ORR activity under the same condition, which again demonstrates the importance of the “N-doping-removal” process in preparing defective carbon catalysts with high ORR activity.

To investigate the reasons behind the good catalytic performance of D-PC-1(900), a series of physical characterizations were conducted. Firstly, XRD and Raman measurements were performed on D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900), respectively, which were prepared either with or without the “N-doping-removal” process. As shown in Fig. 4 a, both catalysts show similar XRD patterns with a broad peak around 2θ≈25°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite, indicative of their graphitized structures. The two carbons also show similar Raman spectra (Fig. 4b) with characteristic peaks at 1327 and 1590 cm−1, which can be assigned to D band and G band, respectively. The D band arises from structural defects, while the G band is closely related to the graphitic carbon phase [42,43]. It has been well established that the carbon defects can be estimated using the ratio of integrated intensities of D band and G band (I
D
/I
G ) [44]. In particular, D-PC-1(900) shows higher I
D
/I
G ratio (2.88) than D-PC-2(900) (2.24). Other catalysts (i.e., D-PC-1(x) and D-PC-3(900), x = 800, 700) prepared with “N-doping-removal” show I
D
/I
G ratio ranging from 2.71 to 2.53 (see Fig. S8), also higher than that of D-PC-2(900). These indicate that (i) all pyrolyzed carbons possess abundant defects and (ii) D-PC-1(900) prepared through the “N-doping-removal process” is slightly more defective than D-PC-2(900).

The structural features of D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900) were further examined using electronic and FT-IR spectroscopy techniques. SEM images (see Fig. S9) suggest that all samples are very porous with abundant macropores. As shown in TEM and HR-TEM images (Fig. 4c, d and Fig. S10), D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900) exhibit similar cotton-like morphologies with abundant meso- and micropores in the carbon matrix. The ring-like patterns shown in the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) images indicate their amorphous nature, in good agreement with the messy lattice fringes observed in HR-TEM image. In addition, D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900) display featureless and similar FT-IR spectra (see Fig. S11). The broad band centered at 1585 cm−1 can be assigned to the in-plane vibration of unsymmetrical C=C moieties [24] and another band at 1100–1280 might be attributed to the presence of a trace of C-O groups [45]. Thus, both these FTIR bands are induced by defects. The fact that D-PC-1(900) shows slightly more intensive such bands might indicate that D-PC-1(900) possesses slightly more defects, in good agreement with Raman analysis.

The porosities of carbon catalysts were evaluated using N2 adsorption-desorption measurements. It was suggested that the N-doped precursors such as N-C-1 and N-C-3 are almost nonporous, whereas all pyrolysis products are very porous. As shown in Fig. 5 , all absorption isotherms exhibit a sharp N2 uptake at low pressure (P/P
0 < 0.001) and a relatively smooth plateau at middle pressure (P/P
0 = 0.001–0.4). Such features indicate that there are abundant micropores inside pyrolysis products, which can be ascribed to the vacancies left by the release of H2O and CO2 during the pyrolysis process [30,46]. In medium to high pressure regions (P/P
0 = 0.4–1.0), all pyrolysis products except for D-PC-1(700) display a small hysteresis loop, suggesting that there are also mesopores in samples, which might be introduced by the efficient removal of N-dopants (in cases of D-PC-1(900, 800) and D-PC-3(900)) or SiO2 template (in case of D-PC-2(900)). Furthermore, these results were verified by calculated pore size distributions (PSD), which reveal that all pyrolysis products except for D-PC-1(700) possess abundant micro- and mesopores (Fig. S12). Their BET specific areas along with the micro- and mesopores areas were calculated using t-plot method and listed in Table 1 . Following information can be extracted: (i) All pyrolysis products except for D-PC-1(700) possess large BET areas over 1200 m2g-1. (ii) Smicro/Smeso ratio is decreasing with the increase of pyrolysis temperature. (iii) D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900) show similar porosities (i.e., BET areas and Smicro/Smeso ratios), which could be attributed to the fact that both carbons were prepared using same precursor (i.e., SA with medium viscosity) and pyrolyzed under same condition. As large BET area and abundant hierarchical porosity are beneficial for the formation of active sites as well as the efficient mass transport in ORR [47], the structural features of pyrolysis products are very helpful in promoting ORR. Nevertheless, it appears that the factor of Smicro/Smeso ratio is less important than others (i.e., BET area and porosity). For instance, D-PC-1(800) shows much higher Smicro/Smeso ratio than D-PC-1(800) (i.e., 3.6 vs 0.6), whereas its ORR activity is only slightly inferior.

Furthermore, the compositions of all samples were explored by means of XPS. The elemental contents estimated by XPS analyses are summarized in Table S5. It is clearly seen that the hydrothermal products N-C-1 and N-C-3 possess moderate N-contents of 5.2 at% and 7.8 at%, respectively, which are mainly pyrrolic and graphitic N species (see Fig. S13). Pyrolysis products possess much lower N-contents (see Fig. 6 ). Particularly, the N-content of D-PC-1(900) is lower than detection limit (<0.10 at%), followed by D-PC-3(900) (0.83 at%), D-PC-1(800) (1.98 at%) and D-PC-1(700) (3.94 at%). XPS analyses reveal the presence of not only pyrrolic, graphitic N but also pyridinic N (see Fig. S14), all of which are ORR-active [48–50]. Note that D-PC-1(900) with negligible N-content shows higher ORR activity than others with higher N-content, which is converse to most literature reports [8,51,52]. Obviously, for D-PC-1(900), the majority of active sites shall be not related to the N-dopants. We therefore propose that the defects generated via N-removal process may play an essential role in promoting ORR. This can be confirmed by a comparative study between D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-2(900). Note that these two carbons bear similar textures and porosities but exhibit very different ORR activities, thus highlighting the essential contribution of the defects generated via the “N-doping-removal” process. As for D-PC-1(800) and D-PC-1(700), the N-removal is less efficient due to lower pyrolysis temperature, thus yielding fewer ORR-active defects and reduced BET area, which might account for their inferior ORR activities relative to that of D-PC-1(900).

Moreover, it is also noteworthy that the two defective carbons D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-3(900), which were prepared using SA with medium and low viscosities as precursors, respectively, show different ORR activities in either alkaline or acidic medium. To better understand how the viscosity factor of precursor polymer (SA) affects the ORR activities of defective carbons, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted on D-PC-1(900) and D-PC-3(900), respectively, in both alkaline and acidic media. The Nyquist plots including experimental (symbols) and fitted data (solid lines) are shown in Fig. 7 . The inset presents the corresponding equivalent circuit, which consists of the electrolyte resistance (Rs), catalyst film resistance (Rc), charge transfer resistance (Rt) and constant phase elements of the catalyst layer and double layer (C1 and C2) [53]. The fitted data are summarized in Table 2 . It can be seen that each Nyquist plot measured in either alkaline or acidic medium is composed of two semicircles at high and low frequencies, which are associated to Rc and Rt, respectively. Obviously, D-PC-1(900) always exhibits smaller Rc and Rt (see Table 2) irrespective of the electrolyte used (i.e., alkaline or acidic), suggesting a higher electron conductivity of the catalyst film and faster charge transfer on the D-PC-1(900)-electrolyte interface. To confirm the EIS results, four-probe conductance measurements were performed. The electronic conductivity was determined to be 1.22 S cm−1 for D-PC-1(900) and 1.08 S cm−1 D-PC-3(900). Therefore, it appears that the viscosity of precursor is an important factor in determining the conductivity of pyrolysis product. The higher conductivity of D-PC-1(900) may account for its superior ORR activity relative to that of D-PC-3(900).

In addition, to better understand the less-explored catalytic mechanism of defective carbon in acidic medium, a series of density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed with Gaussian 09 program package [54]. Particularly, two typical defect models (A and B, see Fig. 8, Fig. S15) with no net spin were employed for theoretical calculations, in which a typical defect (i.e., 585 defect or pentagon defect) [21,23,27] was modeled at the edge of carbon cluster. The control model C (see Fig. S16) containing a pyridinic N was also used for comparison. All model structures were optimized at the M06-2X/3-21G level [55,56] as free species (i.e., in vacuum) and confirmed by vibrational analysis as local-energy minima. The products obtained via key steps of ORR correspond to the lowest calculated potential energy. To evaluate the enthalpy part of the standard Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°) before and after O2 activation, single-point calculations were carried out at the higher level of M06-2X/6–31++G** [57]. According to our calculations, there are two possible pathways toward the formation of A-O-OH+ that is the key intermediate of ORR: (i) protonation of A followed by O2 adsorption and activation; (ii) O2 adsorbed to A followed by proton-induced activation (see Fig. 8 ). The proton affinities as well as the ΔG° for O2 adsorption and activation for models A, B, C are calculated and the results are summarized in Tables S6 and S7. All species show comparable proton affinities, which are also comparable to those known for organic species. 1
1
http://www.kore.co.uk/paffinities.htm. For model A, the defect edge carbon (i.e., C8) plays an important role as the active site in either pathway, where O2 addition and activation favorably occur with the ΔG° of −8.8/-13.6 kcal mol−1 (see Table S7). However, if a simplification of model A with just one pentagon is considered (i.e., model B), the calculations demonstrate the pentagon carbon (i.e., C17) as the most active site, yielding a higher ΔG° value of −21.9 kcal mol−1. As for model C, the carbon (i.e., C17) next to the pyridinic N was revealed to be the most active site for O2 addition and activation, which is in good agreement with literature report [58–60]. The corresponding ΔG° was calculated to be −17.8/-22.6 kcal mol−1, higher than those of model A but comparable to that of model B. These results might indicate that some carbon defects can create active sites for ORR with a smaller or comparable activation barrier relative to that of pyridinic N-doped carbon, which might well account for the considerable ORR activity of the defective carbon D-PC-1(900) in acidic medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term stability of defective carbon catalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the long-term stability of the as-prepared defective carbons, cycling durability tests were performed in both alkaline and acidic media. Particularly, after multiple CV cycles (i.e., 5000 cycles in alkaline medium and 2000 cycles in acidic medium) in a potential range of 0.6–1.0 V (vs. RHE) with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, the E
1/2 of D-PC-1(900) undergoes only 7 mV and 13 mV negative shifts in alkaline and acidic media (Fig. 9 a,b), respectively. In contrast, the potential shift observed for Pt/C is more than 40 mV under the same condition (Fig. S17). These results, therefore, indicate a better stability for D-PC-1(900) relative to that of Pt/C catalyst in either alkaline or acidic media. To confirm these results, chronoamperometric measurements were performed in both alkaline and acidic media. As shown in Fig. S18, D-PC-1(900) exhibits a good stability with a relatively small current decay (i.e., ∼5%) over 5 h in alkaline or over 2.5 h in acidic medium. In comparison, Pt/C catalyst shows a much lower stability with a more remarkable current decay (i.e., ∼20% decay) under the same condition. Moreover, the resistance of D-PC-1(900) to methanol crossover was evaluated by the chronoamperometric measurements at a rotating rate of 1600 rpm in 0.1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9c,d, D-PC-1(900) shows a small current decay (7–15%) upon the addition of 3 M methanol into the electrolyte, irrespective of alkaline or acidic medium, while Pt/C displays a remarkable current drop of ca. 40–70% upon the methanol addition. These results thus suggest a better methanol tolerance for D-PC-1(900) in both alkaline and acidic media.

In summary, a series of defective carbon catalysts were prepared via a simple and scalable “N-doping-removal” approach using abundant seaweed biomass SA as precursor. The systematic studies give rise to the following guidelines: (i) the “N-doping-removal” process is essential in achieving the ORR-active defects; (ii) the defect content, porosity characteristics and conductivity of defective carbon are highly related to the pyrolysis temperature and viscosity of precursor polymer SA; (iii) the optimized defective carbon D-PC-1(900) was obtained via the pyrolysis at 900 °C using the SA with medium viscosity as precursor, which features negligible N-content, abundant ORR-active defects, a high BET surface area (1377 m2 g−1) with a Smicro/meso ratio of 0.6 and good conductivity. In 0.1 M KOH, D-PC-1(900) exhibits excellent ORR activity in terms of an E
1/2 of 0.83 V (vs RHE), comparable to that of Pt/C catalyst. In 0.5 M H2SO4, this catalyst also exhibits considerable ORR activity in terms of an E
1/2 of 0.60 V (vs RHE), which is among the best reported for defective carbons and comparable to many of N-doped carbon catalysts. DFT calculations confirm that the carbon defect can create the active site for ORR in acidic medium. Moreover, experimental results reveal that D-PC-1(900) follows the direct 4e− pathway, indicating efficient reductions of O2 to water in both alkaline and acidic conditions. The catalyst also shows superior stability and methanol tolerance relative to those of Pt/C catalyst. All these results demonstrate that D-PC-1(900) is an excellent candidate for non-precious-metal ORR catalyst in fuel cells. This work also provides more insights to fabricate highly efficient defective carbon catalysts for the ORR in both alkaline and acidic media.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported in part by the Natural Science Foundation of China (51372158, 51772195) and Suzhou Key Laboratory for Advanced Carbon Materials and Wearable Energy Technologies.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.05.007.
